behaviors.description: Request to external API always includes the complete list of valid taxonomy labels
behaviors.justification: The failure mode is in-process: module fails to include all labels in the request dict/payload. This is detectable by mocking the API and inspecting the call arguments. No real API behavior or network needed.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the external classification API. Call classify(document). Assert the mock was called with all expected taxonomy labels (legal, technical, financial, other) present in the request payload.

behaviors.description: When API returns a label not in the taxonomy, module returns 'unknown' instead of forwarding the unexpected value
behaviors.justification: Deterministic error-handling logic. Mock the API to return an out-of-taxonomy label, verify the module sanitizes it to 'unknown'. Failure mode is purely in-process validation.
behaviors.test_description: Mock external API to return label 'spam' (not in taxonomy). Call classify(document). Assert the result is 'unknown', not 'spam'.

behaviors.description: Classification result for the same document is the same across repeated calls
behaviors.justification: The requirement implies the module ensures consistency, likely via caching. Mock the API to return *different* labels on successive calls (e.g., 'legal' then 'technical'). If the module caches the first result, calling classify(doc) twice returns 'legal' both times; if not, it returns 'legal' then 'technical'. This catches the missing cache at Unit level. Mocking controls the API's non-determinism so we test the module's consistency mechanism, not the API's randomness.
behaviors.test_description: Mock external API: first call returns 'legal', second call would return 'technical'. Call classify(same_document) twice. Assert both calls return 'legal' (the cached result).

behaviors.description: Token consumption for a typical document stays within the configured per-call budget
behaviors.justification: This behavior verifies real-world token consumption, not just logic. Mocking token counts only tests budget-enforcement code, but the actual failure mode is 'typical documents consume more tokens than budgeted.' To catch this, we must call the real API with real documents and measure actual token usage. Token counting is API-specific, so mocking misses real behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Call classify(typical_document) using the real external API. Capture the token count from the API response. Verify token_count ≤ configured_budget (e.g., 5000).

behaviors.description: When API signals request rate is too high, module waits and retries rather than returning an error to caller
behaviors.justification: Retry-with-backoff is deterministic logic when mocked. Mock the API to return a rate-limit error (429 or similar), mock time.sleep/asyncio.sleep, call classify(), and verify the module sleeps and retries. Failure modes (no retry, no wait, immediate error) are all caught by mocks.
behaviors.test_description: Mock external API to return HTTP 429 on first call, succeed on second call. Mock time.sleep. Call classify(). Assert sleep was called, and the final result is a successful classification (not an error).
